title: Multi-Robot Task Allocation Framework
desc: Disaster-response coordination framework for multi-agent robots using cvxpy optimization and ROS. 70% efficiency improvement; validated on TACC supercomputing cluster.

detail.summary: A research framework for coordinating multiple robots to complete tasks efficiently in search-and-rescue scenarios. The goal is to assign tasks and plan paths so robots cover more area with less overlap and fairer workload distribution.
detail.why: I wanted to understand how robot teams can work better together. More robots should help, but only if tasks are assigned well. This project explored task allocation using optimization, reinforcement learning, and fairness so the system could be faster while keeping work balanced across robots.
detail.problem: Scaling path-planning optimization to many robots within real-time constraints. Traditional approaches become computationally intractable at scale, and naive allocation overloads some robots while others sit idle.
detail.approach: The system defines robots, tasks, and environment. Task allocation is solved using cvxpy, minimizing travel cost, overlap, and unfair workload distribution. Assignments are passed into ROS where robots follow their assigned tasks. The system is validated in Gazebo simulation before deployment.
detail.status: Completed. Presented at UT Austin, SCCUR, and STARS 2024.
detail.results: The framework improved task allocation efficiency by about 70% and reduced planning time by about 45% compared to baseline methods.

sections.title: What I learned
sections.body: Multi-robot systems are not just about movement. They are also about decision-making, coordination, and fairness. A good system should not only finish quickly, it should also avoid overloading one robot while others do less.

sections.title: Broader applications
sections.body: These ideas connect to distributed systems, swarm robotics, logistics, and large-scale scheduling. They also apply to future work in embedded and real-world robotics where decisions need to be made quickly with limited resources.
